elements.sent-2411-001: The study of systems described by a Yang-Mills (YM) action augmented with a $\theta$-term has provided, historically, several interesting results.
elements.sent-2411-002: Classically speaking, the addition of a $\theta$-term does not alter the classical equations of motion.
elements.sent-2411-003: Yet, its theoretical and phenomenological implications are far reaching, once the quantum fluctuations are taken into account.
elements.sent-2411-004: The $\theta$-dependence lies at the heart of the Witten--Veneziano solution of the $\eta$-$\eta'$ puzzle~\cite{Witten:1979vv, Veneziano:1979ec,Kawarabayashi:1980dp,Witten:1980sp and it is the starting point for the Peccei--Quinn hypothetical solution to the strong-CP problem based on axions.}
elements.sent-2411-005: Accordingly, the study of $\theta$-dependence in a variety of different settings has attracted a great deal of attention in the past years.
elements.sent-2411-006: Starting from QCD, SU($N$) and Sp($2N$) models~\cite{Coleman:1985rnk, Gross:1980br,Schafer:1996wv in $4d$, to $CP^{N-1}$ models~\cite{DAdda:1978vbw,Witten:1978bc and U(N) models~\cite{Kovacs:1995nn,Bonati:2019ylr,Bonati:2019olo in $2d$, to quantum mechanical models~\cite{Jackiw:1979ur,Gaiotto:2017yup, Bonati:2017woi .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-007: So far, the main target of investigation has been the free energy of the system and its $\theta$-dependence, both at zero and finite temperature.
elements.sent-2411-008: Using analytical methods, it is possible to obtain theoretical predictions for the $\theta$-dependence of the QCD vacuum energy either using effective theories close to the chiral limits for low temperatures~\cite{DiVecchia:1980yfw, diCortona:2015ldu , or for the $\theta$-dependence of the QCD free energy via perturbative and semi-classical arguments for asymptotically-high temperatures~\cite{Gross:1980br,Schafer:1996wv,Boccaletti:2020mxu .}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Away from these regimes, the $\theta$-dependence of the vacuum energy has been investigated by means of numerical Monte Carlo simulations of the lattice-discretized theory, both in QCD~\cite{Bonati:2015vqz, Petreczky:2016vrs,Frison:2016vuc,Borsanyi:2016ksw,Bonati:2018blm,Burger:2018fvb,Chen:2022fid,Athenodorou:2022aay and in $\SU(N)$ pure-gauge theories~\cite{Alles:1996nm,Alles:1997qe,DelDebbio:2004ns,DelDebbio:2002xa,DElia:2003zne,Lucini:2004yh,Giusti:2007tu,Vicari:2008jw,Panagopoulos:2011rb,Bonati:2013tt,Ce:2015qha,Ce:2016awn,Berkowitz:2015aua,Borsanyi:2015cka,Bonati:2015sqt,Bonati:2016tvi, Bonati:2018rfg, Bonati:2019kmf , with particular focus on the large-$N$ limit of these models, due to its relation with the Witten--Veneziano mechanism~\cite{Witten:1979vv, Veneziano:1979ec,Kawarabayashi:1980dp,Witten:1980sp .}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: Concerning two-dimensional models, the $\theta$-dependence of $2d$ $\mathrm{CP ^{N-1}$ models is amenable to be exactly computed using analytical methods in the large-$N$ limit up to the next-to-leading-order in the $1/N$ expansion~\cite{DAdda:1978vbw, Campostrini:1991kv, DelDebbio:2006yuf,Rossi:2016uce, Bonati:2016tvi , and such predictions have been verified to be supported by numerical evidence~\cite{Vicari:1992jy,Bonanno:2018xtd,Berni:2019bch,Bonati:2019olo,Bonati:2019ylr .}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: The $\theta$-dependence of $2d$ $\mathrm{CP ^{N-1}$ models has also been studied numerically~\cite{Berni:2020ebn,Bonanno:2022dru in the limit $N\to2$, as these theories reduce to the $2d$ O(3) non-linear $\sigma$ model in this limit (see also~\cite{Nogradi:2012dj,Alles:2014tta ), whose $\theta$-dependence is theoretically interesting due to its connection with the Haldane conjecture.}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Finally, also the $\theta$-dependence of $2d$ $\mathrm{U (N)$ Yang--Mills theories can be exactly computed analytically~\cite{Kovacs:1995nn, Bonati:2019ylr, Bonati:2019olo .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: The $\theta$-dependence of the spectrum of the theory has received comparatively less attention, with only one exploratory study~\cite{DelDebbio:2006yuf present in the literature.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Our main goal is to bridge that gap.
elements.sent-2411-015: In particular, we report on our study~\cite{Bonanno:2024ggk of the $\theta$-dependence of the spectrum of glueballs and fluxtubes of pure-gauge $\SU(N)$ models in $4d$.}
elements.sent-2411-016: The results that we have obtained constitute a substantial improvement over the previously available results.
elements.sent-2411-017: This could be achieved with the combination of the imaginary-$\theta$ method and of the Parallel Tempering on Boundary Conditions (PTBC) algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-018: The former enables us to improve the signal-to-noise ratio compared to the standard Taylor expansion approach, and the latter enabled us to avoid the freezing of topology, especially at large-$N$.
elements.sent-2411-019: This proceeding is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-020: In Sec.~\ref{sec:setup we describe our numerical setup.}
elements.sent-2411-021: In Sec.~\ref{sec:res we present the main results of~\cite{Bonanno:2024ggk .}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Finally, in Sec.~\ref{sec:conclu we draw our conclusions.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Direct lattice simulations of the Yang--Mills theory at non-zero values of $\theta$ are hindered by the infamous sign problem, as the topological term is purely imaginary, thus yielding a complex action.
elements.sent-2411-024: A popular technique to bypass the sign-problem is to resort to simulations of imaginary-values of $\theta_{_\I \equiv \mathrm{i}\theta$, characterized by a purely-real action.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Assuming analyticity around $\theta=0$ it is possible to use analytic continuation and infer the dependence on the real parameter $\theta$ from the observed dependence on the imaginary one $\theta_{_\I}$, at least for small enough values of $\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-026: The imaginary-$\theta$ method has been shown to be extremely effective in improving the signal-to-noise-ratio of the higher-order coefficients in the $\theta$-expansion of several quantities, compared to simulations at $\theta=0$ alone~\cite{Bhanot:1984rx, Azcoiti:2002vk, Alles:2007br, Imachi:2006qq,Aoki:2008gv,Panagopoulos:2011rb, Alles:2014tta, DElia:2012pvq, DElia:2013uaf,Bonati:2015sqt, Bonati:2016tvi, Bonati:2018rfg, Bonati:2019kmf,Bonanno:2018xtd, Berni:2019bch, Bonanno:2020hht, Bonanno:2023hhp .}
elements.sent-2411-028:
For the lattice topological charge, we employed the standard clover discretization,  eq Q_{\clov =  rac{1 {2^9 \pi^2 \sum_{\mu
uho\sigma = \pm1 ^{\pm4 \varepsilon_{\mu
uho\sigma \Tr\left[ \mathcal{P _{\mu
u (x) \mathcal{P _{ho\sigma (x) ight]~.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: As a result, the lattice parameter $\theta_\L$ is thus related to the physical one by: $\theta = \mathrm{i Z_{_Q \theta_\L$.}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Obtaining the renormalization constant $Z_{_Q}$ requires the calculation of the lattice topological charge.
elements.sent-2411-031: This can be computed by means of smoothing.
elements.sent-2411-032: To this end one can adopt any of the various algorithms that have been proposed in the literature, such as cooling~\cite{Berg:1981nw,Iwasaki:1983bv,Itoh:1984pr,Teper:1985rb, Ilgenfritz:1985dz,Campostrini:1989dh,Alles:2000sc , smearing~\cite{APE:1987ehd, Morningstar:2003gk , or gradient flow~\cite{Luscher:2009eq,Luscher:2010iy, Luscher:2011bx , as they have been shown to be all numerically equivalent when matched to one another~\cite{Alles:2000sc, Bonati:2014tqa,Alexandrou:2015yba .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: In this work, we determine the lattice topological charge and the renormalization factor $Z_{_Q}$ via cooling as follows~\cite{Panagopoulos:2011rb : \beq Z_Q = \frac{\braket{Q_{_\I Q_\clov {\braket{Q_{_\I ^2 , \quad Q_{_\I = \mathrm{round}\left\{\alpha \, Q_\clov^{(\cool)}\right\}, \eeq with $Q_{_\I}$ the lattice integer-valued topological charge~\cite{DelDebbio:2002xa , $Q_\clov^{(\cool)}$ the clover-discretized charge computed after 20 cooling steps, and $\alpha$ the numerical value minimizing \beq \left\langle\left( \alpha Q_\clov^{(\cool) - \mathrm{round}\left\{\alpha \, Q_\clov^{(\cool)}\right\} \right)^2\right\rangle, \quad 1 < \alpha < 2.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: For the $N=3$ runs, we adopted the standard 4:1 mixture of over-relaxation~\cite{Creutz:1987xi and heat-bath~\cite{Creutz:1980zw, Kennedy:1985nu algorithms.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: For $N=6$ runs we instead adopted the Parallel Tempering on Boundary Conditions (PTBC) algorithm~\cite{Hasenbusch:2017unr to overcome the severe critical slowing down experienced at large-$N$ and close to the continuum limit by topological modes~\cite{Alles:1996vn,DelDebbio:2004xh, Schaefer:2010hu , known as topological freezing.}}
elements.sent-2411-036: The PTBC algorithm has been extensively applied both in $2d$ models~\cite{Hasenbusch:2017unr,Berni:2019bch, Bonanno:2022hmz and in $4d$ gauge theories, both with~\cite{Bonanno:2024zyn and without~\cite{Bonanno:2020hht,Bonanno:2022yjr,DasilvaGolan:2023cjw,Bonanno:2023hhp,Bonanno:2024nba dynamical fermions.}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: This algorithm consists in simulating several replicas of the lattice, all differing from each other by just for the boundary conditions imposed on a small sub-region of the lattice, known as the defect.
elements.sent-2411-038: Boundary conditions are taken to be periodic everywhere but on the defect where, on each replica, they are chosen to interpolate between periodic and open.
elements.sent-2411-039: The state of each replica is evolved with a standard local Monte Carlo updating algorithm except for swaps between gauge configurations of the different replicas, that are proposed and stochastically accepted/rejected via a standard Metropolis step.
elements.sent-2411-040: The calculation of observables is always performed on the replica with periodic boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-041: This algorithm enables us to enjoy the fast decorrelation of topological modes achieved with open boundaries (which is transferred towards the periodic replica by the swaps) and, at the same time, to avoid the systematic effects introduced with open boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-042: We determine the $\SU(N)$ spectrum with the variational method.
elements.sent-2411-043: An appropriate variational basis of zero-momentum projected operators $\left\{O_i(t)\right\}$ was defined for each symmetry channel of interest.
elements.sent-2411-044: In particular, the operators were path ordered products of link variables along space-like paths of various shapes and sizes, and at different levels of blocking and smearing~\cite{BERG1983109,APE:1987ehd,TEPER1987345, Teper:1998te,Lucini:2001ej,Lucini:2004my,Blossier:2009kd, Lucini:2010nv, Bennett:2020qtj,Athenodorou:2020ani, Athenodorou:2021qvs .}
elements.sent-2411-045: Their correlator was computed on the generated ensembles, \beq C_{ij (t) = \braket{O_i(t)O_j(0) \eeq and then a Generalized EigenValue Problem (GEVP), $C_{ij (t) v_j = \lambda(t,t_0)C_{ij (t_0)v_j$, was solved.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: This enabled us to find the optimal combination of operators for each symmetry channel, and to define the corresponding correlation function, \beq \overline{C (t) = C_{ij (t)\overline{v _j, \eeq with $\overline{v}$ the eigenvector related to the largest eigenvalue $\lambda(t,t_0)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: The GEVP was always solved for $t_0=a$, and we checked in a few cases that $t_0=2a$ gave compatible results.
elements.sent-2411-048: The ground state mass was then obtained via a best fit of $A \left[ \exp(-mt)+\exp(-m(aL-t)) \right]$ to the correlator, using $m$ and $A$ as fitting parameters, over a range of $t$ where the effective mass, defined as follows, \beq a m_{_{\mathrm{eff (t) = - \log\left[\frac{\overline{C (t+a) {\overline{C (t)}\right]~, \eeq exhibited a plateaux.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: For the mass gap of the theory, we considered a variational basis made of 4-, 6- and 8-link operators in the $\mathrm{A _1$ representation of the octahedral group.}
elements.sent-2411-050: For the torelon mass we used a variational basis made of products of fat-links winding around the time direction once.
elements.sent-2411-051: We then extracted the string tension using~\cite{deForcrand:1984wzs (with $L$ the lattice size in lattice units): \beq a^2\sigma = \frac{a m_{\tor {L + \frac{\pi {3 L^2 .. \eeq.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: We determined the mass gap of the theory (i.e., the mass of the lightest glueball state) and the string tension in lattice units for several values of $\beta$ and $\theta_{\L}$.
elements.sent-2411-053: The $\theta$ dependence was parameterized by Taylor expanding in up to the next-to-leading order, around $\theta=0$, \beq am_{\G (\theta) = am_{\zpp \left[1 + m_2 \theta^2 + \mathcal{O (\theta^2)\right], a^2\sigma(\theta) = a^2\sigma \left[1 + s_2 \theta^2 + \mathcal{O (\theta^2)\right]~, \eeq where the well-established fact that at $\theta=0$ the mass of the $0^{++}$ glueball is the lightest one in pure-Yang--Mills theories~\cite{Lucini:2004my, Athenodorou:2020ani, Athenodorou:2021qvs,Vadacchino:2023vnc ) was used.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Using analyticity, and the renormalization property of the lattice imaginary-$\theta$ parameter, one simply has: \beq am_{\G (\theta_{\L ) = am_{\zpp}\left[ 1 - m_2 Z_{_Q ^2\theta_\L^2 + \mathcal{O (\theta_\L^2)\right], a^2\sigma(\theta_{\L ) = a^2\sigma\left[ 1 - s_2 Z_{_Q ^2\theta_\L^2 + \mathcal{O (\theta_\L^2) \right].}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: Since the value of $Z_{_Q (\beta)$ was known for each $\beta$, we could obtain $m_2$ and $s_2$.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Our results for $m_2$ and $s_2$ at $N=3$ are displayed in Fig.~\ref{fig:ex_fit_theta_N3 .}
elements.sent-2411-057: They are very stable as a function of the fitting range, signaling that we are insensitive to the effects of higher-order corrections.
elements.sent-2411-058: We also display a few examples of plateaux of effective masses.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[scale=0.35]{cont_limit_mass_N_3} \includegraphics[scale=0.35]{cont_limit_s2_and_m2} \caption{Continuum limit of our $N=3$ results for $m_2$ and $s_2$. We also computed the continuum limit of $m_{\zpp}/\sqrt{\sigma}$, which is in perfect agreement with the previous result of~\cite{Athenodorou:2020ani}.} \label{fig:ex_cont_limit_N3}
elements.sent-2411-059: In Fig.~\ref{fig:ex_cont_limit_N3 we display the continuum extrapolation of our results for $m_2$ and $s_2$.}
elements.sent-2411-060: We also extrapolated towards the continuum limit the dimensionless ratio $m_{\zpp /\sqrt{\sigma}$ in order to verify that our result is in agreement with the previous determination provided in Ref.~\cite{Athenodorou:2020ani .}}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[scale=0.35]{large_N_COMP_m2} \includegraphics[scale=0.35]{large_N_COMP_s2} \caption{Continuum scaling of $N^2 m_2$ and $N^2 s_2$ for $N=3$ and $N=6$.} \label{fig:ex_cont_limit_N6}
elements.sent-2411-061: These are our final determinations for $N=3$, in the continuum limit, \beq \frac{m_{\zpp {\sqrt{\sigma = 3.398(25), \qquad \text{(continuum limit, $N=3$) , m_2= -0.0083(23), \qquad \text{(continuum limit, $N=3$) , s_2= -0.0258(14), \qquad \text{(continuum limit, $N=3$) .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: However, our data allowed for a first quantitative check of the following expected large-$N$ scaling: \beq m_2 = \frac{\overline{m _2 {N^2 + \mathcal{O}\left(\frac{1 {N^4}\right),\quad s_2 = \frac{\overline{s _2 {N^2 + \mathcal{O}\left(\frac{1 {N^4}\right).}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: Thus, in the end we can quote the following estimates: \begin{equation}\label{eq:bar_res} \overline{s}_2 \simeq -0.23(1)\ ,\quad \overline{m}_2 \simeq -0.075(20)\ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-064: The present manuscript reports on the main finding of~\cite{Bonanno:2024ggk .}
elements.sent-2411-065: We have studied the $\theta$-dependence of the $\SU(N)$ spectrum, focusing on the mass gap of the theory and on the string tension for $N=3$ and $N=6$.
elements.sent-2411-066: For $\SU(3)$, in the continuum limit we found: \beq \frac{m_{\G {\sqrt{\sigma }\Bigg\vert_{\theta=0 = \frac{m_{\zpp {\sqrt{\sigma = 3.398(25)~,\quad m_2 = -0.0083(23),\quad s_2 = -0.0258(14), \eeq where \beq m_2\equiv \frac{1 {2m_{\zpp }\times \frac{\mathrm{d m_{\G (\theta) {\mathrm{d}\theta^2}\Bigg\vert_{\theta\,=\,0 ,\quad s_2\equiv \frac{1 {2\sigma}\times \frac{\mathrm{d}\sigma(\theta) {\mathrm{d}\theta^2}\Bigg\vert_{\theta\,=\,0 .}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: As a result, the coefficient $g_2$ of the order $\mathcal{O (\theta^2)$ correction to the dimensionless ratio $m_\G/\sqrt{\sigma}$, \beq \left(\frac{m_\G {\sqrt{\sigma }\right)(\theta) = \frac{m_\zpp {\sqrt{\sigma }\left(1+g_2\theta^2+\mathcal{O (\theta^4)\right), \eeq is compatible with zero within two standard deviations, \beq g_2 = m_2 - \frac{s_2 {2 = 0.0046(24)~.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Actually, we can provide a counter-example to this.
elements.sent-2411-069: In Refs.~\cite{DElia:2012pvq,DElia:2013uaf, Bonanno:2023hhp , the $\theta$-dependence of the $\SU(N)$ deconfinement critical temperature $T_c$ was investigated for $N\ge 3$ (for a first SU(2) study see~\cite{Yamada:2024pjy ).}}
elements.sent-2411-070: It was concluded that if \beq T_c(\theta) = T_c[1 - R \theta^2 + \mathcal{O (\theta^4)]~, \eeq then \beq R(N=3) = 0.0178(5)~,\quad R(N)= \frac{0.159(4) {N^2 +\mathcal{O}\left(\frac{1 {N^4}\right) \quad (N>3)~.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: At a fixed value $Q$ of the topological charge, the following approximate relation holds for the mass $M$ of any bound state, \beq \frac{M^{(Q) - M {M \approx \frac{M_2 {2 \chi V ~, \eeq where $\chi=\braket{Q^2 /V$ is the topological susceptibility and $M_2$ is the order $\mathcal{O (\theta)^2$ coefficient of $M(\theta)=M[1+M_2\theta^2+\mathcal{O (\theta^4)]$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: For $N=3$ the topological susceptibility is roughly $\chi\simeq (1\text{ fm )^{-4}$.}
elements.sent-2411-073: For a standard lattice volume of $V=(1.5\text{ fm )^4$, one has the following bound on the systematic error on the numerical estimation of the lightest $0^{++}$ glueball: \beq \frac{\Delta m_{\zpp {m_{\zpp }\Bigg\vert_{N=3 \approx \frac{m_2 {2\chi V \approx -0.08\%.}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-077: Using analytical methods, it is possible to obtain theoretical predictions for the $\theta$-dependence of the QCD vacuum energy either using effective theories close to the chiral limits for low temperatures~\cite{DiVecchia:1980yfw, diCortona:2015ldu,Guo:2015oxa,Lu:2020rhp , where the $\theta$-dependence of the vacuum energy essentially stems from the $\theta$-dependence of the pion mass (the $\theta$-dependence of the mass of some light resonances has also been investigated in~\cite{Acharya:2015pya ); or for the $\theta$-dependence of the QCD free energy via perturbative and semi-classical arguments for asymptotically-high temperatures~\cite{Gross:1980br,Schafer:1996wv,Boccaletti:2020mxu .}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: The $\theta$-dependence of the spectrum of the theory has received comparatively less attention, with only one exploratory lattice study~\cite{DelDebbio:2006yuf present in the literature.}
elements.sent-2411-079: The ground state mass was then obtained via a best fit of $A \left[ \exp(-mt)+\exp(-m(La-t)) \right]$ to the correlator, using $m$ and $A$ as fitting parameters, over a range of $t$ where the effective mass, defined as follows, \beq a m_{_{\mathrm{eff (t) = - \log\left[\frac{\overline{C (t+a) {\overline{C (t)}\right]~, \eeq exhibited a plateaux.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: Finally, we recall the interesting role played by $m_2$, identified in~\cite{Brower:2003yx, Aoki:2007ka in the estimation of the systematic error introduced in lattice spectra calculations performed at fixed topological sector.}
